text: We routinely paint the patient with betadine and then we clean it with any alcohol based solutions.
The whole area is properly cleaned.
Any intercostal drain that is present in the patient before surgery is also removed at this time.
A thin film is also applied over the incision area where we need to do the surgery.
Anatomical landmarks in the nearby areas are also exposed so that the incision can be properly planned.
So in this way the post-trial thoracotomy incision area is kept exposed.

going to mark the incision points the first point is marked around two finger beds below the inferior angle of the scapula and the posterior side as you can see between the posterior border of the scapula and the midline that is a vertebral spines one point is marked and anteriorly this incision is extended in the line of the ribs actually and the anterior most point is marked so in this way this the three points are marked
Thereafter the incision is given.
The incision actually extends in the lateral part and in the posterior part of the chest wall.
That is why it is called as posterolateral thoracotomy incision.
See how from anterior to posterior this incision extends.
After cutting the skin, the deeper layers that is subcutaneous tissue, the fascias and the muscles, these things are all cut with the help of an electrocautery.
Now as you can see, we are using electrocautery to cut the deeper layers.
Care should be taken that the skin is not burnt.
Once the rib cage is reached, after reaching the rib cage, here you can see in this picture we are cutting the muscles.
Once the muscles are cut, we reach the rib cage.
Now once the rib cage is reached, we need to decide that from which intercostal space we will be entering into the pleural cavity.
For this we need to aspirate.
Sometimes in cases of loculated empyema this aspiration helps.
If the empyema is involving whole of the pleural cavity then we routinely open it from the 5th intercostal space.
But if the empyema is loculated then we aspirate first and we localize from which intercostal space it is good to open up.
Because otherwise if we open too high or if we open too low then operating might be difficult.
Now here we are cutting the intercostal muscles of the intercostal space from where we have decided to enter the pleural cavity.
The intercostal spaces are very narrowed down because of the chronic empyema, because of lot of fibrosis and adhesions.
It's difficult to open up the chest in such patients.
Slowly the chest is being opened here as you can see with the help of the finger guiding the finger so that the underlying lung and those structures are not injured.
Once we get some space in between the ribs we use the chest spreader to open it up slightly.
The slow opening is done if we do too much of
harsh or forceful opening we might injure the ribs.
There might be rib fractures.
So we opened up slowly.
As you can see there are lot of adhesions.
The underlying parietal pura is thickened and fibrous and it is adhered to the rib cage from inside.
So we are trying to release the parietal pura from the under surface of the rib.
One rib below and one rib above.
From the space from where we have opened, one rib below and one rib above, we release the paratalsura which is attached or which is adhered to the under surface of the rib.
Now, once we open up the...
Once we have cut the parietal pleura, as you know that this is actually a very classic picture that is seen after thoracotomy in an empyema patient.
So once the chest wall is opened, that is rib cage is cut, the intercostal spaces are cut, we have reached the thickened parietal pleura and then the thickened parietal pleura is also cut.
While cutting the thickened parietal pura, lot of care needs to be taken because the underlying lung might be adhered to the parietal pura sometimes.
If the lung is not adhered, there is an empyema cavity is there inside the parietal pura.
In those situations, after cutting the parietal pura, we reach the empyema cavity.
So now, empyema cavity is actually a space, it's a cavity.

which is between the parietal pura and the bistral pura and it is filled with pus, debris and other necrotic materials so this is a very classic picture that we encounter once we open up the empyma cavity and sometimes when the pus is dried up when there is very little necrotic material present we might not get this kind of picture in those situations

we may simply have a after cutting the parietal pura we may simply hit the parenchyma of the lung and in those situations the empyema cavity is actually dried up and it contains nothing and the parietal pura and the visceral pura actually fuse together and

once we open up the there is no plane in between the parietal and visceral pleura so after opening up the parietal pleura we actually reach the visceral pleura that is opened and we encounter the lung so in those situations one needs to be very careful so that the underlying lung is not injured while opening the pleura otherwise this is the most common picture that we encounter once we open up the empyema cavity after cutting the parietal pleura
Now, once we have removed all the necrotic materials and the debris from the empyema cavity, then we encounter this kind of picture.
So now this is again a picture which we encounter after opening.
You can see the underlying lungs very slowly expanding.
It is being restricted by the lot of debris, clots and those materials overlying the lung which is preventing it from complete expansion.
So we are trying to release
Those materials, remove those materials.
We are trying to cut the thickened visceral pleura so that the underlying lung gets expanded.
This process is done all around the lung from the apex to the base to the diaphragmatic surface, all around the lung anteriorly up to the pericardium, posteriorly up to the paravatribal gutter.
It takes a lot of time, lot of patience.
One should not injure the underlying lung while doing so because then the chances of postoperative recovery gets very bad.
Now you can see in this image, once you have removed the thickened visceral pura, what happens?
Once you remove the thickened visceral pura, the underlying lung that is trapped actually looks like this.
get expanded very well and then they fill the residual empyema cavity in the post operative phase
This surgery requires lot of patience.
You cannot do this surgery in a very hurried fashion.
If you do it in that way, you will end up resulting lot of injuries to the lung.
There will be air leaks.
There will be formation of small bronchopulmonary fistulas.
There can be major damage to the lung resulting into a major air leak.
And all these air leaks will prevent the expansion of the lung in the post-operative.
period.
As you can see here, the visceral pleural peel is being very carefully removed.
At times, it's very difficult to find a plane between that peel and the lung parenchyma surface.
The lower lobes, the middle lobe is now very well expanded as you can see here.
The interlobal fissures, it's also very important to release these fissures in between the lobes because then the lung will get expanded well.
The aim is to fully free the lung so that a fully freed lung actually fills up the empyema cavity in a very nice way.
Here you can see the healthy lung is expanding very well and it's very satisfying to see this result once the surgery is over.
We routinely do many decortication procedures in our CTBS unit in Institute of Medical Sciences, Banaras Hindu University.
Most of the times these patients are post-tubercular which is still very very common in our area.
Many of these patients are post-pneumonic also, especially children.
We operate lot of empyemas in children also.
Sometimes, very rarely, it's very difficult to find a plane between the thickened visceral tura and the lung parenchyma.
In such cases, when we are not able to find any plane and we are not able to release the lung, we actually make small, small...

turtle cages like we call it kind of turtle cage operation as we do in pericardectomy sometimes when we are not able to find the plane between the pericardium and the myocardium in the same way in the lung we release the lung like this we make small small incisions so you can see the lung popping out in between these thickened visceral tura fields it's a kind of burfi we call it as burfi katna so
You can see in between these things.
So this requires a lot of precision and lot of patience by making these kind of turtle cages over the lung fan camel surface so that the lung gets released a bit.
This is not a kind of complete release but there is some release.
So this is a picture that you can see after a successful decortication.
The lung is expanded very well and we all feel very happy after this result.
All the debris and the angles are cleared.
The interlobar fissures are freed.
There is no air leak.
It's safe not to breach the parietal pleura there and stay in the plane between the parietal and the visceral pleura.

and all after the surgeries overall the bleeding is carefully checked and any major air leaks should be repaired in decortication surgeries in our setting over our experience has been that it's not very necessary to remove the thickened parietal pura which is lying adhered to the rib cage actually
We remove the thickened visceral pleura that has trapped the lung, the lung parenchyma.
So our target is to remove that thickened visceral pleura.

We leave the thickened parietal pleura as such remaining attached to the underlying rib cage except for removing it, say for example, two or three ribs above and below the incision line, above and below the site from which the intercostal space we have entered.
Removing the parietal pleura from higher, from lower down, from other places is not very much needed and we have seen that it doesn't give any extra advantage.
Instead it may cause lot of bleeding while trying to remove the parietal pleura from the under surface of the rib cage.
There might be lot of bleeding because the intercostal vessels give lot of collaterals to these and there are lot of angiogenesis in this area because of the inflamed

pleura so there might be lot of bleeding lot of torrential bleeding which is difficult to control in this setting so we leave the parietal pleura as such except for the area from where we have entered the chest one or two ribs above the intercostal space from where we have entered we remove that parietal pleura but the visceral pleura needs to be removed from all around the lung

and one more thing that during this surgery is we routinely use double lumen endotracheal tube so that our anesthetics they are very expert in putting the double lumen endotracheal tubes so that the other lung remains guarded during this surgery the other lung doesn't get flooded with the pus secretions or the blood that may go into the other lung during the surgical procedure
So once the surgery is over patients are shifted to our ICU.
We don't ventilate these patients in the ICU because they can be extubated very easily.
Our anesthetics are highly expert in this thing and they extubate patients on table itself and we have never encountered any kind of problem later in the ICU because of early extubation protocols.
